findings.title: NoneType comparison crash: `num_401_calls` initialized to `None`, compared with `< 2`
findings.detail: In `init_per_thread_state()` (line 78), `self.tl.num_401_calls` is initialized to `None`. In `__call__` (line 221), it is set to `1` before the hooks fire — but `handle_401` and `handle_redirect` can also be invoked from redirect/response hooks registered on *previous* requests in the same thread that were never reset. More critically, the `handle_redirect` hook (line 168) sets `num_401_calls = 1`, and `handle_401` reads `num_401_calls = self.tl.num_401_calls` (line 177) and then evaluates `num_401_calls < 2` (line 180). If somehow `num_401_calls` is still `None` (e.g. hooks fired before `__call__` sets it, or a redirect fires before a proper call cycle), this will raise `TypeError: '<' not supported between instances of 'NoneType' and 'int'` in Python 3. The original code used `getattr(self, 'num_401_calls', 1)` as a safe fallback (old line 175), but that safety net has been removed. While the common path is safe, the `None` sentinel is dangerous and should be replaced with an actual integer default (e.g. `1`) in `init_per_thread_state`.

findings.title: New threaded test silently passes when `ThreadPool` import fails and does not assert status code
findings.detail: Two issues: (1) The entire test body is inside `if ThreadPool is not None:` (line 430), so on Python 2 environments where `multiprocessing.pool.ThreadPool` is unavailable (which is actually unlikely since it exists in Python 2.7+), or if the import fails for any reason, the test silently does nothing and vacuously passes. The import fallback `ThreadPool = None` (lines 37–38) makes this even more opaque. (2) The `do_request` function only asserts that `'"auth"' in r.request.headers['Authorization']` but never checks `r.status_code == 200`. A response that results in a 401 (authentication failure) would not have an `Authorization` header at all, which would raise a `KeyError` rather than a clean assertion failure. The test should assert `r.status_code == 200` explicitly.

findings.title: Per-thread state initialized only once per thread — stale nonce/challenge reused across independent requests on pooled threads
findings.detail: The `init_per_thread_state()` guard (`if not hasattr(self.tl, 'init')`) means that for a thread that has already handled a request, `last_nonce`, `chal`, `nonce_count`, etc. are **never reset** across subsequent requests on the same thread. In a thread pool (e.g. with `ThreadPool(processes=50)` but actually threads), thread objects are reused. A thread that handled request A will reuse `last_nonce` and `chal` from request A when it handles request B at a later point in time. This can result in `build_digest_header` being called with a stale nonce or realm, leading to authentication failures or incorrect behavior. The original code reset all fields on every `__call__` invocation; the new code only resets `num_401_calls` (line 221). The fields `last_nonce`, `nonce_count`, `chal`, and `pos` should also be reinitialised on each new request (in `__call__`) rather than just once per thread lifetime. Note: reusing `last_nonce` across calls is intentional for nonce reuse optimisation, but only when the nonce is still valid — state from a completely different request may refer to a now-expired nonce on a different server.

findings.title: Hooks registered on every `__call__` without deduplication — multiple hooks accumulate on session reuse
findings.detail: Every call to `__call__` registers `handle_401` and `handle_redirect` via `r.register_hook(...)` (lines 219–220) without checking whether they are already registered. This is not new with this PR (it was the same before), but the thread-local refactoring makes the consequence more subtle: when the same `HTTPDigestAuth` instance is reused in a session over many requests, each request's `PreparedRequest` will get one additional copy of the hooks appended. Since hooks are stored on the `PreparedRequest` rather than the session, this only matters per-request — but if a session's default hooks somehow accumulate (depending on how `register_hook` works in the session context), multiple `handle_401` invocations per response could fire, causing double-increments of `num_401_calls` and corrupted state. This is worth verifying.

findings.title: `hash_utf8` may be used uninitialized if algorithm is not MD5, MD5-SESS, or SHA
findings.detail: This is a pre-existing issue not introduced by this PR, but worth noting: if `_algorithm` is something other than `'MD5'`, `'MD5-SESS'`, or `'SHA'`, `hash_utf8` is never assigned. The subsequent `KD = lambda s, d: hash_utf8(...)` at line 106 would then fail with `UnboundLocalError` when `KD` is actually called (not at definition). The guard `if hash_utf8 is None: return None` at line 108 would never catch this because `hash_utf8` is unbound, not `None`. This was present before the PR.

findings.title: Threaded test depends on live external service (httpbin.org) — not a unit test
findings.detail: The new `test_DIGESTAUTH_THREADED` test uses `httpbin('digest-auth', 'auth', 'user', 'pass')` which requires a live network connection to httpbin.org (or whatever `HTTPBIN_URL` is set to). Unlike a proper unit/mock test, this means CI failures can occur due to network flakiness, rate-limiting by httpbin.org, or environment misconfiguration. The threading behaviour that this PR aims to fix should be testable with a lightweight mock server or by mocking the `requests.Session` layer. All other digest auth tests share this problem, but this particular test fires 100 concurrent requests which is significantly more aggressive.

findings.title: `handle_redirect` accesses `self.tl` without `init_per_thread_state()` guard
findings.detail: `handle_redirect` writes `self.tl.num_401_calls = 1` (line 168) but `init_per_thread_state()` is only called from `__call__`. If, through some unusual flow (e.g. the hook fires on a thread that never called `__call__`, or the object was passed between threads), `self.tl` would not have `num_401_calls` set, and reading it later in `handle_401` would raise `AttributeError`. Similarly `handle_401` reads `self.tl.pos` and `self.tl.num_401_calls` at lines 173 and 177 without any guard. The fix is to call `init_per_thread_state()` at the start of both hook methods.

findings.title: Missing space around `=` in keyword argument / assignment
findings.detail: `session.auth=auth` (line 424) is missing spaces around `=`. Should be `session.auth = auth` per PEP 8.
